extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property G(opted_out implies not sent) requires that no reachable state ever has opted_out=true and sent=true.

Transition [t5] directly breaks this: on send_attempt, if (opted_out and ticks >= 12), it sets sent:=true — while opted_out is true. So if ticks can reach 12 and opted_out is true at a send_attempt, t5 is enabled and an execution exists in which it fires, producing opted_out=true AND sent=true.

Reaching ticks=12 requires 12 tick events (ticks starts at 0, increments by 1 per tick, capped at 12). tick also sets sent:=false but does not touch opted_out.

Concrete violating execution (14 events, within the 16-event bound):
- Initial: opted_out=F, sent=F, ticks=0
- tick x12 (t4 each time): ticks=12, sent=F, opted_out=F
- optout (t1): opted_out=T, sent=F, ticks=12
- send_attempt: both [t3] (guard opted_out) and [t5] (guard opted_out and ticks>=12) are enabled. The property must hold on EVERY reachable execution; the execution in which [t5] fires sets sent:=true, yielding opted_out=T and sent=T — a violation.

Note the non-determinism between t3 and t5 when ticks>=12: even if t3 (sent:=false) might fire under some priority scheme, t5 is a satisfiable enabled transition, so a violating execution is reachable. No priority is declared that disables t5, so the property does not hold over the entire input/execution space.
